title: pair
description: Formalizing .first and .second from map: bundling exactly two values together, and storing pairs inside other containers.
objectives: Understand pair as a simple bundle of exactly two values
Know how pairs compare to each other

sections.text: In map, iterating gave us entries as pair objects: pair.first for the key, pair.second for the value. Let's look at pair directly, on its own.
pair<T1, T2> bundles exactly two values, possibly of different types, into a single object with two members: .first and .second.

Both forms work identically — {} brace initialization and the make_pair() helper function. pair comes from <utility>, though it's commonly available already through headers like <map> that use it internally.
This combines vector from earlier with pair — a very common DSA pattern for holding a list of related two-value items, like (name, score), (index, value), or (row, column).

pair supports comparison operators out of the box: it compares .first first, and only looks at .second to break a tie if .first is equal — this is called lexicographic comparison, the same idea as alphabetical dictionary ordering.

sections.title: A luggage tag with a name and a destination
sections.text: A luggage tag holds exactly two pieces of information glued together — a passenger's name and their destination city. pair works the same way: two related values, bundled as one unit, wherever it's more convenient to carry them together than as two separate loose variables.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting .first and .second are the actual member names — not .key/.value or .0/.1.
Assuming pair's two types must match — pair<string, int> mixes types freely, just like map's key and value types.
Forgetting #include <utility> (though it's often pulled in transitively by headers like <map>).
Trying to compare pairs of genuinely unrelated types with < or > when .first types don't support ordering — comparison relies on both element types being comparable.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is pair, and what are its two members called?
questions.answer: pair<T1, T2> bundles exactly two values into one object, with members named .first and .second.

questions.question: How do you create a pair?
questions.answer: Using brace initialization, like pair<string, int> p = {"Aman", 20};, or the make_pair() helper function.

questions.question: How does C++ compare two pairs with < or >?
questions.answer: Lexicographically — comparing .first first, and only comparing .second to break a tie if .first is equal.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: pair<T1, T2> bundles exactly two values, possibly of different types, into one object with members .first and .second — exactly what map returns when iterated. It can be created with brace initialization or make_pair(), and is commonly stored inside other containers, like vector<pair<string, int>>, for lists of related two-value items such as (name, score) or (index, value). Pairs support comparison operators out of the box, comparing .first first and using .second only to break a tie, following the same logic as alphabetical dictionary ordering.

sections.title: Trick to Remember pair
sections.text: pair = a luggage tag, two glued-together values. .first and .second, never .key/.value. Compares .first first, .second breaks ties.
